question:
A-1. Let \( f(n) \) be the sum of the first \( n \) terms of the sequence \( 0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4, \cdots \), where the \( n \)th term is given by
\[
a_{n}=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
n / 2 & \text { if } n \text { is even } \\
(n-1) / 2 & \text { if } n \text { is odd }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Show that if \( x \) and \( y \) are positive integers and \( x>y \) then \( x y=f(x+y)-f(x-y) \).
solution:
A-1 It is easily verified by induction that
\[
f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}
n^{2} / 4 & \text { when } n \text { is even } \\
\left(n^{2}-1\right) / 4 & \text { when } n \text { is odd. }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore, since \( x+y \) and \( x-y \) always have the same parity, in any case we must have
\[
f(x+y)-f(x-y)=\frac{(x+y)^{2}-(x-y)^{2}}{4}=x y .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
A-1. Let \( totalsum(indexvar) \) be the sum of the first \( indexvar \) terms of the sequence \( 0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4, \cdots \), where the \( indexvar \)th term is given by
\[
sequenceterm=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
indexvar / 2 & \text { if } indexvar \text { is even } \\
(indexvar-1) / 2 & \text { if } indexvar \text { is odd }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Show that if \( largerint \) and \( smallerint \) are positive integers and \( largerint>smallerint \) then \( largerint \, smallerint=totalsum(largerint+smallerint)-totalsum(largerint-smallerint) \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-1 It is easily verified by induction that
\[
totalsum(indexvar)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}
indexvar^{2} / 4 & \text { when } indexvar \text { is even } \\
\left(indexvar^{2}-1\right) / 4 & \text { when } indexvar \text { is odd. }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore, since \( largerint+smallerint \) and \( largerint-smallerint \) always have the same parity, in any case we must have
\[
totalsum(largerint+smallerint)-totalsum(largerint-smallerint)=\frac{(largerint+smallerint)^{2}-(largerint-smallerint)^{2}}{4}=largerint \, smallerint .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A-1. Let \( honeycomb(thumbtack) \) be the sum of the first \( thumbtack \) terms of the sequence \( 0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4, \cdots \), where the \( thumbtack \)th term is given by
\[
rainforest_{thumbtack}=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
thumbtack / 2 & \text { if } thumbtack \text { is even } \\
(thumbtack-1) / 2 & \text { if } thumbtack \text { is odd }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Show that if \( snowflake \) and \( peppercorn \) are positive integers and \( snowflake>peppercorn \) then \( snowflake peppercorn=honeycomb(snowflake+peppercorn)-honeycomb(snowflake-peppercorn) \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-1 It is easily verified by induction that
\[
honeycomb(thumbtack)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}
thumbtack^{2} / 4 & \text { when } thumbtack \text { is even } \\
\left(thumbtack^{2}-1\right) / 4 & \text { when } thumbtack \text { is odd. }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore, since \( snowflake+peppercorn \) and \( snowflake-peppercorn \) always have the same parity, in any case we must have
\[
honeycomb(snowflake+peppercorn)-honeycomb(snowflake-peppercorn)=\frac{(snowflake+peppercorn)^{2}-(snowflake-peppercorn)^{2}}{4}=snowflake peppercorn .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A-1. Let \( voidtotal(noncount) \) be the sum of the first \( noncount \) terms of the sequence \( 0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4, \cdots \), where the \( noncount \)th term is given by
\[
collection=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
noncount / 2 & \text { if } noncount \text { is even } \\
(noncount-1) / 2 & \text { if } noncount \text { is odd }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Show that if \( tinyvalue \) and \( hugevalue \) are positive integers and \( tinyvalue>hugevalue \) then \( tinyvalue\,hugevalue=voidtotal(tinyvalue+hugevalue)-voidtotal(tinyvalue-hugevalue) \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-1 It is easily verified by induction that
\[
voidtotal(noncount)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}
noncount^{2} / 4 & \text { when } noncount \text { is even } \\
\left(noncount^{2}-1\right) / 4 & \text { when } noncount \text { is odd. }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore, since \( tinyvalue+hugevalue \) and \( tinyvalue-hugevalue \) always have the same parity, in any case we must have
\[
voidtotal(tinyvalue+hugevalue)-voidtotal(tinyvalue-hugevalue)=\frac{(tinyvalue+hugevalue)^{2}-(tinyvalue-hugevalue)^{2}}{4}=tinyvalue\,hugevalue .
\]

garbled_string.question:
A-1. Let \( jskdplqw(zmpxnght) \) be the sum of the first \( zmpxnght \) terms of the sequence \( 0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4, \cdots \), where the \( zmpxnght \)th term is given by
\[
bdwqjtuv_{zmpxnght}=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
zmpxnght / 2 & \text { if } zmpxnght \text { is even } \\
(zmpxnght-1) / 2 & \text { if } zmpxnght \text { is odd }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Show that if \( qrhtmpas \) and \( ksjdupal \) are positive integers and \( qrhtmpas>ksjdupal \) then \( qrhtmpas ksjdupal=jskdplqw(qrhtmpas+ksjdupal)-jskdplqw(qrhtmpas-ksjdupal) \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-1 It is easily verified by induction that
\[
jskdplqw(zmpxnght)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}
zmpxnght^{2} / 4 & \text { when } zmpxnght \text { is even } \\
\left(zmpxnght^{2}-1\right) / 4 & \text { when } zmpxnght \text { is odd. }
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore, since \( qrhtmpas+ksjdupal \) and \( qrhtmpas-ksjdupal \) always have the same parity, in any case we must have
\[
jskdplqw(qrhtmpas+ksjdupal)-jskdplqw(qrhtmpas-ksjdupal)=\frac{(qrhtmpas+ksjdupal)^{2}-(qrhtmpas-ksjdupal)^{2}}{4}=qrhtmpas ksjdupal .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let the sequence \((b_n)_{n\ge 1}\) be given by
\[
   b_n = \bigl\lfloor \tfrac{n-1}{2} \bigr\rfloor \qquad(n=1,2,3,\dots),
\]
so that the first few terms are \(0,0,1,1,2,2,3,3,\dots\).  For a positive integer \(N\) define
\[
   g(N)=\sum_{k=1}^{N} b_k.
\]
Prove that for any non-negative integers \(a, b\) with \(a\ge b\) one has
\[
   ab \,=\, g\bigl(a+b+1\bigr)\; -\; g\bigl(a-b+1\bigr).
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Closed form for g.  We first derive an explicit expression for g(N).
   
      g(N)=\sum_{k=1}^{N} \Bigl\lfloor\frac{k-1}{2}\Bigr\rfloor.
   
   Write N=2m or 2m+1.
   * If N=2m the summands occur in m pairs (0,0),(1,1),\ldots ,(m-1,m-1); hence
   
       g(2m)=2\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}j= m(m-1)=\frac{(2m)^2-2\,(2m)}{4}=\Bigl\lfloor\frac{(2m-1)^2}{4}\Bigr\rfloor.
   * If N=2m+1 there are the same m pairs plus a last term m, so
   
       g(2m+1)=m(m-1)+m=m^2=\frac{(2m)^2}{4}=\Bigl\lfloor\frac{(2m)^2}{4}\Bigr\rfloor.
   Thus in every case
   
      \boxed{\;g(N)=\Bigl\lfloor\dfrac{(N-1)^2}{4}\Bigr\rfloor\;.}

2.  Parity observation.  For given non-negative integers a\geq b the numbers
   A=a+b+1 and B=a-b+1 satisfy A\equiv B(mod 2) because A-B=2b is even.  Hence the same branch of the piecewise formula for g (even or odd argument) applies to both A and B.

3.  Insert the closed form.  Because A and B have the same parity we may drop the floor symbols when taking the difference:
   
      g(A)-g(B)
      =\frac{(A-1)^2-(B-1)^2}{4}.\tag{1}
   
4.  Difference of squares.  Expand (1):
   \[(A-1)^2-(B-1)^2=((A-1)-(B-1))\bigl((A-1)+(B-1)\bigr)= (A-B)(A+B-2).\]
   Now substitute A=a+b+1, B=a-b+1:
   \[
      A-B = 2b,\qquad
      A+B-2 = (a+b+1)+(a-b+1)-2 = 2a.
   \]
   Therefore
   \[(A-1)^2-(B-1)^2 = (2b)(2a)=4ab.\]
   Dividing by 4 in (1) gives
   \[g(A)-g(B)=ab.\]

5.  Conclusion.  Since A=a+b+1 and B=a-b+1, we have established
   \[\boxed{\;ab = g(a+b+1)-g(a-b+1)\;}\],
   as desired for all non-negative integers a\geq b.

core_steps: Note x+y and x−y share parity, so same branch of f applies.
Insert x+y and x−y into the closed form.

slot1.description: Assumption that x and y are strictly positive; non-negativity suffices for the proof.
slot1.original: x and y are positive integers

slot2.description: Requirement x>y; equality case x=y can be permitted without affecting any step.
